note: January wraps Almora in fog and frost. The Kumaoni hill town drops to 2-5°C, pine forests drip with moisture, and the Himalayan views — when the clouds part — are startling in their winter clarity.
January mornings at Bhimtal drop to 3-5°C and the lake turns mirror-still.
January Bomdila is a cold, crystalline world.
January Kalimpong is cold and clear. Delo Hill offers razor-sharp Kanchenjunga views at dawn, but the nurseries — the town's soul — are dormant.
January wraps Landour in fog and frost, turning Ruskin Bond's village into something from one of his own winter stories.
January Mussoorie is bone-cold and nearly deserted.
January Nainital is cold, quiet, and occasionally dusted with snow.
January freezes Pfutsero to the bone — India's coldest Naga town wears frost like a uniform.
January freezes Phawngpui to its bones — Mizoram's highest peak wears frost and offers views that end at Myanmar.
January Tsomgo is a frozen mirror at 3753m — the lake is solid ice framed by snow-dusted slopes.
January freezes Ukhrul into silence — the Tangkhul Naga hills under winter-sharp skies.
January Yuksom is a ghost town in the best way — you own the trails and the silence.
February in Almora is cold but brightening.
February is Bhimtal's coldest month, regularly touching 1-2°C before dawn.
February brings Losar — Tibetan New Year — to Bomdila's monasteries, and the celebrations are intimate and real.
February Dirang adds festival energy to winter charm.
February in Landour occasionally delivers snow—not guaranteed, but when it comes, this tiny cantonment looks like a Victorian Christmas card.
February continues the dry window. Warming slightly.
February is January with slightly better odds.
February is the tail end of Nainital winter.
February keeps Pfutsero frozen but adds longer days — more light for the highland panoramas.
February keeps Phawngpui frozen but photogenic — the summit rewards early starts and warm layers.
February Tsomgo stays frozen and adds occasional fresh snow for drama.
February holds Ukhrul in cold storage — but the Tangkhul villages don't hibernate.
February is Yuksom's secret — the first rhododendrons are budding while everyone else waits for March.
The forest dries and the falls reduce to…
March sees the snow begin retreating from Lolab Valley's lower stretches.
March heats Moreh's bazaar — the border trade doesn't slow but your stamina might.
March sees the last flamingos departing — catch them while you…
March teases Ukhrul with warmth — the hills start greening but the Shirui Lily isn't ready yet.

April transitions Bhalukpong from safari to river town.
Heat makes boat safaris tiring but crocs love the…
The monolith absorbs heat — the climb becomes an oven…
The open temple grounds bake — early morning…
April settles Champhai after Chapchar Kut — the festival energy fades but the spring beauty doesn't.
The phenomenon continues but heat makes seabed walks…
Only resident birds and dolphins — the migration spectacle is…
Losing its cool advantage — still better than the…
The hilltop bakes — quick visit from Bhubaneswar…

Beach heat builds — only dawn/dusk visits…
April tests your heat tolerance — Haflong trades cool charm for sticky warmth.
Losing its cool advantage — still better than the…
The Deccan sun bakes the granite city — Charminar shimmer is real but…
April heats Jorhat up but Bihu keeps the energy higher than the temperature.

The delta heat makes boat rides shorter and less…

Heat reduces the lake appeal — boating in midday is…
April quiets Lunglei after the festival — spring warmth without the crowds.
The forest canopy provides shade but comfort…
The open fort bakes — cathedral is a cool…
April bakes Moreh into a border-town sweat — but the market stalls don't melt.
The treeless island bakes — bring water and sun…
April begins Namdapha's transition. Rain touches the canopy, humidity thickens, and the forest shifts gear — butterflies explode in number, but so do leeches.
April heats Neermahal into shimmer — the palace waves in the hot air above the lake.
April Nongriat tests your commitment. The 3000 steps in 35-degree humidity separate the serious from the Instagram tourists.
April heats Pasighat up. The lowland location (153m) means it gets properly warm, and humidity climbs.
The banyan is its own air conditioner — a 4-acre shade…
Heat thins the crowds but temple visits need early morning…
Heat thins the crowd — intimate exploration of the floating…
Heat makes the gorge a convection channel — early morning…
April Shnongpdeng is hot river country. The clarity fades slightly as pre-monsoon rains upstream start adding sediment.
April warms the corridor but onward roads stay open.
April begins the thaw — -2 to 10°C. Snow retreats from the village to higher slopes.
April is Sivasagar sweating through its biggest party — Bihu is worth the heat if culture moves you.
Heat sets in but the Kalamkari workshops run…
April is Tamenglong between seasons — winter's clarity fading, monsoon's drama building.
Hot springs in hot weather — less therapeutic…
April Tura is sticky. At 349m with no altitude relief, the heat and humidity make everything slower — including your enthusiasm for uphill walks.
April warms Ukhrul into its festive season — Tangkhul celebrations build toward the Lily's arrival.
April heats Unakoti into a steam bath — the rock carvings sweat as much as you do.
The cave interior is a relief but the exposed hillock…
The Krishna basin traps heat — temple visits best at…
The heat and humidity make outdoor exploration…
The fort grounds bake in April sun — early morning…
May is Alchi's sweet spot for most travellers.
Late May sometimes opens the first trail access, though snow patches remain across the meadow.
May brings peak summer tourism to Bhimtal as plains dwellers escape 45°C Delhi for 28-32°C lakeside air.
May warms Champhai toward monsoon — the hills green up and the thunder rehearses.
May is Haflong sweating — the hill station altitude isn't enough to dodge Assam's pre-monsoon heat.
May pushes Jorhat into sauna mode — but the tea bushes don't care and neither do the pluckers.
May is when Lamayuru hits its stride. Clear skies, temperatures between 8-18°C, and the Leh highway in good shape.
May is when Likir becomes genuinely comfortable.
May greens Lunglei up for monsoon — the last comfortable month before the rain commits.
May is a gamble at Manas — the park might close any day once the first serious rain hits.
May Mawphlang builds toward monsoon drama.
May is Mon exhaling after Aoling — the villages quiet down but the hospitality doesn't.
May tests your commitment to Moreh — 36°C at the border and rising.
May tests your devotion to Neermahal — the heat is punishing but the pre-storm skies are cinematic.
May Nongriat is a sauna with stone steps.
May turns Sariska into a heat mirage — the Aravallis shimmer, the rock radiates, and only the most determined safari-goers remain.
May Shnongpdeng is the last month you can reasonably camp by the Umngot.
May in Siliguri is hot, but that's the point — you're heading uphill.
May bakes Sivasagar into slow motion — even the monuments seem to sweat.
May primes Tamenglong for monsoon — the waterfalls preview what's coming, and what's coming is a lot.
May Tura is the Garo Hills sweating through their last dry weeks.
May bakes Unakoti's hillside — the rock faces endure another summer like they've endured 1300 others.

The Godavari begins its annual swell — the temple town…
June starts dry and hot (30-33°C) before the monsoon arrives around June 20th.

The Mahanadi rises ominously — Cuttack's annual flood watch…
Monsoon greens the battlefield — dramatic…

June marks the monsoon's arrival, usually by the second week.
Monsoon light in the cathedral is Gothic and…
Monsoon muddies the village but workshops keep…
The monsoon transforms the coastline — dramatic but not…
Monsoon greens the Kakatiya ruins — atmospheric but…

Monsoon Lingaraj is atmospheric — wet stone…
The forests drip with monsoon moisture — trails are…
July lake is full but access is limited — beautiful from…
July monsoon arrives in Lolab. Rainfall is moderate compared to the rest of Kashmir, but trails get muddy and leeches appear in the forest.

The looms never stop — monsoon is irrelevant to the…

July monsoon reaches Lahaul in diminished form — rain shadow means 50-80mm versus 500mm on the Manali side.

The Krishna swells past the barrage — dramatic but…
The lake temple looks magnificent against monsoon…

The Buddha statue against monsoon clouds is…

The river at full spate is dramatic but…
August shows the caves at their most dynamic — water…
The egg-fort glistens in the rain — beautiful from…
August maintains monsoon patterns but with increasing gaps.
The canyon walls drip with monsoon green — dramatic but…
August mist makes the colonial buildings look like English…
August Old City Ramzan food trail is legendary — haleem, kebabs,…
When the Godavari is in full spate the delta becomes an inland…
The 13 islands are at their greenest — if you can get…
The temple looks dramatic against monsoon…
August continues the monsoon but the valley has absorbed it—everything is lush, full, dripping.
August under the monsoon banyan is a primeval…
August workshops prepare Dussehra-season…
August brings the full Godavari flood — the barrage gates are a…
August brings the most dramatic monsoon…
August Hirakud at full capacity is engineering at its most…
August continues monsoon — 150-200mm. Landslide risk on the approach road peaks.
August continues Sissu's peak season — warm (12-24°C), green, and busy by Lahaul standards.
The quiet monsoon months offer intimate temple…
August continues the monsoon magic — fewer pilgrims mean shorter…
The caves look magnificent against monsoon…

September sees Chhau training resume for the winter…
September sees cautious reopening — green mangrove at maximum…
September Bhopal is emerald but damp. The upper lake is at maximum and the boat rides are dramatic with monsoon clouds.
September is Bomdila shaking off the monsoon.
September sees the clean tidal recessions…
September boats resume on a full, green Chilika — first migrants…
September coolness returns — the hill station…
September sees the first trekkers return — trails still…
September peels the clouds back from Dirang.
September seas start calming — cautious…
September starts opening up. The fort hill is green, the crowds haven't returned, and you might catch a clear day that makes the climb worthwhile.
September starts clearing. Kanchenjunga peeks through clouds for the first time in months, and when it does, the view from Delo Hill makes you forget the mud on your shoes.
September sees tribal markets cautiously…
The first cool nights return — a preview of the winter…
The town dries out and the artisans return to outdoor…
September sees the first eco-lodges reopening — trails are muddy but…
September Mawphlang is the monsoon loosening its grip.
September is Mechuka rebuilding. The second half can surprise you — clouds lift, the valley emerges washed and vivid, and the silence after months of rain has a physical quality.
September starts the transition. Waterfalls still flow strong but rain windows open up.
September starts drying out. The hill is emerald green and the crowds haven't returned.
September sees the first boats cautiously return to the…
September sees the first dry days. The Visva-Bharati campus is emerald and quiet.
September starts reopening the hill roads.
September sees the sand re-emerge from monsoon…
September springs start steaming as air cools — the magic…
September sees the rain ease but the Shipra stays full.
October is Alchi's last accessible month for most travellers.
October closes Bangus. The first serious snowfall can come any day.
October is a coin flip at Gurudongmar. The views when it works are the best of the year — sharp, cold, electric.
October Lachen shakes off the monsoon and the mountains reappear.
October brings the last comfortable window before winter shuts Lamayuru down again.
October at Likir is autumn in the high desert—sharp air, clear skies, emptying village.
October opens Lunglei back up — not fully dry, but dry enough to justify the drive from Aizawl.
October brings the advance guard — flamingo numbers build…
October reopening reveals Simlipal at its greenest — trails are muddy but waterfalls…
October cautiously reopens the Sundarbans.
November brings the first freeze — 0-12°C, frost on the orchards, the harvest winding down.
November freezes Sissu — -5 to 5°C. Snow arrives.
November dries Tamenglong into trekking condition — the trails firm up and the waterfalls step down from October's peak.
December brings Almora's coldest temperatures — 0-3°C at night, frost on morning walks — but also its clearest skies.
December Bhimtal is cold, clear, and nearly empty.
December Bomdila is a monastery town in winter — stripped down, cold, and quietly beautiful.
December brings Dirang full circle — back to hot springs and cold air, monasteries and mountain silence.
December Kalimpong does Christmas properly — it has a significant Christian population.
December Landour is winter again, full circle.
December Christmases Lunglei into community warmth — every church, every home, every hill village.
December cools Moreh further — the border town wraps up the year with quieter trade and holiday warmth.
December Mussoorie is a snow gamble wrapped in Christmas packaging.
December splits into two Nainitals. Early December (1-20) is cold, grey, and empty — a continuation of November's gloom at 4-8°C daytime.
December returns Pfutsero to the deep freeze — Naga Christmas hymns rising through frost.
December frosts Phawngpui's summit meadows into crunchy white — the Blue Mountain at its most alpine.
December chills Tamenglong into quiet beauty — the wildest corner of Manipur under winter stillness.
December Tsomgo begins its annual freeze.
December Christmases in Ukhrul are Tangkhul Naga first, global second — hymns in local tongue, feasts on local terms.
December Yuksom retreats into winter silence.
December settles Ziro into winter mode. The valley is stark and beautiful, village life turns inward, and the cold keeps all but the committed away.
